MyAIM Central — The Vault
Build & Create 45–60 min read
App Building Tutorial

I spent months and real money learning what NOT to do. Here's what actually works.

The process that produced a Best of Show vibeathon winner, a working family management app, and a 145+ table platform — all by a self-taught mom of nine.

No coding background required Real process, real results Includes prompt templates
The Honest Part

The "build an app in one day" tools almost worked. That's the most frustrating kind of failure.

Before I found a process that works, I spent months and real money on the "build an app with no code in one day" tools. And they almost worked. I'd describe what I wanted, the tool would generate something beautiful — a clean dashboard, a professional layout. I'd think, "This is it!"

Then I'd try to actually use it. Nothing behind that pretty login page worked. I'd ask the tool to fix one thing, and it would break two other things. Fix. Break. Regenerate. Break something else. Watch credits disappear. Start over.

Some tools ate through $50–100 in a single afternoon, and I'd have nothing to show for it except a nice-looking landing page that couldn't save data. I tried multiple platforms. Same story every time.

What I learned — the hard, expensive way — is that the tools weren't the core problem. The problem was that I was asking them to figure out what I wanted while building it. I was designing and constructing at the same time, and every design change meant a construction teardown.

This guide is what I wish someone had handed me before I spent that time and money.

"AI tools are brilliant builders. They're terrible at guessing what you want. Your job is to be the world's clearest communicator."

The Big Idea

Think of it like building a house. You wouldn't start framing before you have blueprints.

If you hired a brilliant contractor to remodel your kitchen, you wouldn't just say "make it nice." You'd show them the layout, pick the cabinets, choose the countertops, decide where the outlets go, and agree on a budget before anyone picks up a hammer. The more clearly you communicate, the better the result — and the less money you waste on teardowns.

AI coding tools work exactly the same way. They're extraordinary at building. They need you to be extraordinary at describing.

This guide walks you through the process we use — the same process that consistently produces quality, production-ready apps. It's not the fastest way to get something on screen. It's the fastest way to get something good on screen.

The Process

Seven phases from idea to live app.

Here's the full journey. Each phase builds on the last — skipping ahead is where things go sideways.

1
1–3 hours

Know What You're Building

Clarify your vision before you open any tool. Answer five specific questions that will save you weeks of rework. Write the answers down — you'll paste them into your first AI conversation.

2
Days to weeks — this is where quality is won or lost

Write Your Feature Specs (PRDs)

Describe every feature in detail before building any of them. This is the phase most people skip. It's the phase that makes the biggest difference.

3
A few hours

Audit Everything

Have AI read all your specs together and look for contradictions, gaps, and missing connections. Fix issues before any code gets written.

4
1–2 hours

Set Up Your Workshop

Get your tools installed and your AI builder oriented. There's one setup step most people miss — and it changes everything about how well the AI understands your project.

5
The fun part

Build in Cycles

Plan a small piece, build it, test it, update your notes. Repeat. Four steps that repeat until your app is done.

6
Ongoing

Test Like a User

Use your app the way a real person would. Only a human can check: does this feel right? Would my target user understand this without instructions?

7
An afternoon

Ship It

Put it on the internet. With the right setup, deployment is almost automatic — and there's a security check prompt you should run before you go live.

Phase 1

Five questions that will save you weeks of rework.

Before you open any AI tool, sit down for an hour and answer these. Write your answers down — you'll paste them into your first AI conversation.

01

Who is this for?

Not "everyone." One specific person with one specific problem. "Homeschooling moms who are drowning in paperwork" is a million times more useful than "parents."

02

What should it feel like?

Pick 2–3 words. Calm and organized? Fun and encouraging? Our words were "warm, capable, and never guilt-inducing." That single direction influenced hundreds of small decisions.

03

What's the ONE thing it does?

If someone used your app for 30 seconds, what would they walk away understanding? You might have 50 features in mind. That's fine. But you need one clear core.

04

What should it NOT be?

"Don't make it look like a generic dashboard." "Don't make it feel clinical." Clear boundaries help your AI enormously — and they'll come up in every feature decision.

05

What are your non-negotiables?

For us: "every AI suggestion goes through the user before it's saved" and "no guilt language, ever." Name yours early — they'll come up in every feature decision.

Your First Prompt

I'm planning an app and I want your help thinking through the architecture before we write any code. Here's my vision: [paste your answers to the five questions above]. I'd like you to help me create a system overview. Ask me clarifying questions before you start drafting — I'd rather get the foundation right than rush into details.

The key phrase: "ask me clarifying questions." You want your AI to push back and surface things you haven't thought about before you start building.

Phase 2

This is the phase most people skip. It's the phase that makes the biggest difference.

A PRD is a Product Requirements Document. That sounds corporate and intimidating, but it's really just a detailed description of one feature in your app. Think of it as a blueprint for one room in your house.

Why PRDs matter: Without them, you tell your AI "build a task management feature." It builds something. Then you tell it "now build a calendar." It builds a calendar that doesn't know your tasks exist. Then you build a notification system that doesn't know about either one. Three features, zero connection between them.

With PRDs, you describe all three features before building any of them. The connections are designed, not discovered.

What makes a good PRD:

  • What is this feature and why does it exist?

    One paragraph. What problem does it solve? Who uses it?

  • What can the user actually do?

    Walk through every screen, every button, every action. Be specific enough that someone who's never seen your app could build it from your description.

  • Who can see and do what?

    A simple permissions table. Mom sees everything. Teen sees only their tasks. Child sees only their assigned activities.

  • What data gets stored?

    What fields does this feature need in the database? What connects to other features?

  • What does "done" look like?

    A specific, checkable list. "User can create a task. Task appears in the list. Task can be marked complete. Completed tasks move to history." Specific enough to test.

"For our current project, we wrote over 50 PRDs — 137 planning documents total — before a single line of code was written. Your app probably needs 5–10. The principle is the same: describe everything first, then build."

User stories help: "As a mom, I want to see all my family's tasks in one place so I can make sure nothing falls through the cracks." "As a teenager, I want to see only my own tasks so I'm not overwhelmed by the whole family's to-do list." Writing these makes you think like a user, not a builder.

Phase 3

Have AI read everything you wrote and look for the cracks.

Once all your PRDs are written, have your AI read them all together and look for problems. This is the audit phase — and it's worth every minute.

The Audit Prompt

I've written PRDs for my entire app. Read all of them and check for: - Contradictions between any two features - Data that flows between features where the receiving feature doesn't acknowledge it - Permission rules that are inconsistent or have gaps - Naming inconsistencies (same concept called different things in different PRDs) - Features that reference something that doesn't exist For each issue, tell me: what the conflict is, which documents are affected, and your recommended resolution.

Fix issues by updating the actual PRDs — not by just noting them somewhere. You want clean, internally consistent specs before any code gets written.

Phase 4

The tools you need — and the one setup step most people miss.

What You NeedRecommendedAlternatives
Code editor VS Code Free Cursor, Windsurf
AI coding tool Claude Code Recommended GitHub Copilot, Cursor AI
Database + backend Supabase Recommended Firebase, PocketBase
Version control GitHub Free GitLab
Hosting Vercel Free tier Netlify, Railway

The step most people miss: CLAUDE.md

Before your AI coding tool writes anything, it needs to understand your project's rules. We use a file called CLAUDE.md that lives in the root of the project. Your AI coding tool reads this file automatically at the start of every session.

Think of it as the employee handbook for your AI builder. It contains your conventions, your database structure, a registry of what's built and what's planned, and any rules the AI should always follow.

What to Put in CLAUDE.md

## Project Overview [One paragraph describing what this app does and who it's for] ## Tech Stack - Frontend: [your framework] - Backend: Supabase (auth, database, edge functions) - Hosting: Vercel ## Conventions - [Any naming conventions you want the AI to follow] - [Any patterns established in the codebase] - [Any rules: "always use TypeScript", "never store API keys in client code"] ## Database Structure [List your main tables and their key fields] ## Feature Registry - [x] Authentication (complete) - [x] User roles (complete) - [ ] Task management (in progress) - [ ] Calendar (planned) ## Current Session Goal [Update this at the start of each session with what you're building today]
Phase 5

Four steps that repeat until your app is done.

The build phase follows a simple four-step cycle for every feature. Once you get the rhythm, it feels almost meditative.

A

Plan the Build Prompt

With your planning AI, create a detailed build prompt for your coding tool. Include what's already built, which PRD this implements, and a verification checklist.

B

Build It

Paste the prompt into your AI coding tool. Let it work. Watch for errors — it should flag and fix compilation problems as it goes.

C

Test It

Open your app and try the feature. Start with the happy path, then test the edge cases. Screenshots are your best friend — paste them directly into the conversation.

D

Update Your Notes

After each build cycle, update CLAUDE.md with any new patterns established and mark the feature as completed.

Build order matters:

  • Design system first

    Colors, fonts, spacing, component styles. Everything else builds on this.

  • Authentication second

    Login, signup, password reset. You need this before you can test anything user-specific.

  • User roles and permissions third

    Who can see and do what. This affects every feature that comes after.

  • Navigation and layout fourth

    The shell of the app. Where does everything live?

  • Core features in dependency order

    Build features that other features depend on first.

Phases 6 & 7

Test like a real user. Ship when it's ready.

Testing isn't a phase that happens once at the end. It happens throughout, at different levels:

  • While building

    Does this feature work? Does it match the PRD?

  • At milestones

    Step back and use the whole app as a real user would. Does the flow make sense?

  • Before launch

    Have someone who wasn't involved in building use the app. Watch them. Where they get confused, that's a UX problem. Where they get frustrated, that's a priority fix.

Only a human can check: does this feel right? Would my target user understand this without instructions? Does the app feel like a product or a homework assignment?

Pre-Deployment Security Check

Before I deploy this app, check: - Are there API keys written directly in any code file? - Is the .env file in .gitignore? - Can a regular user access admin-only pages? - Are passwords stored securely (never in plain text)? - Are login attempts rate-limited? - Is user data protected by Row Level Security in Supabase? For each item, tell me the status and flag anything that needs to be fixed before going live.

You don't need to understand all of these — your AI coding tool does. But running this prompt before deployment means you're not accidentally publishing your API keys to the public internet.

Adding AI Features

Your app doesn't contain an AI. It talks to one.

If your app includes AI-powered features, here's how it actually works. Your app talks to an AI that lives somewhere else — the same way it talks to a database. The conversation happens through an API.

  • Sign up for an AI service

    OpenAI, Anthropic, or a router like OpenRouter (which gives you access to multiple models through one API).

  • Get your API key

    This is your password to the service. It goes in your .env file — never in your code.

  • Build a proxy function

    When a user asks your app's AI a question, it goes to your server (an Edge Function), which adds your API key and forwards it to the AI service. The AI responds, your server passes the answer back. The user never sees your API key.

AI Feature Setup Prompt

I want to add AI-powered [describe the feature] to my app. Set up an Edge Function that acts as a proxy — the frontend sends requests to our Edge Function, which adds the API key and forwards to the AI service. Store the API key in environment variables, never in client-side code.

Important: AI features cost money per use. Every conversation turn might cost $0.01–0.05. This is why model routing matters — simple tasks to cheaper models, complex tasks to more capable ones.

A Note Before You Start

Sometimes a brilliant spreadsheet is exactly what you need.

Before committing to the full build process, it's worth knowing that the major AI assistants now have built-in ways to create small, functional tools without any setup:

Claude Artifacts
Interactive mini-apps right in the conversation. Calculators, planners, visualizers, simple games. Great for personal tools or prototyping.
Google Gemini Gems
Custom AI assistants and multi-step workflow tools you build by describing what you want in plain language.
ChatGPT Custom GPTs
Specialized versions of ChatGPT with custom instructions, uploaded knowledge files, and specific behaviors.
ChatGPT Canvas
Interactive, shareable web content — apps, games, infographics — directly from conversation.

These are perfect when you need something for yourself, when you're prototyping, or when the tool is simple enough that it doesn't need user accounts or persistent data. Once you need multiple users with different access levels, data that grows over time, or features that connect to each other — that's when you need the full process.

"Artifacts and Gems are like making a great spreadsheet. The full build process is like building the software that replaces the spreadsheet. Both are valuable. Know which one you need."

What I Actually Use

My personal tech stack — and why.

This isn't the only way. But it's a proven combination where every piece works well with every other piece, costs are reasonable, and the learning curve is manageable for a non-technical builder.

  • 💬
    Planning & Architecture

    Claude (claude.ai with Max subscription)

    For PRDs, design decisions, build prompts, and architecture thinking. The best thinking partner I've found.

  • 💻
    Code Editor

    VS Code

    Free, runs on any computer, best extension ecosystem. The industry standard for a reason.

  • 🤖
    AI Coding Tool

    Claude Code

    Reads CLAUDE.md automatically, builds incrementally, makes targeted changes. Understands context better than anything else I've tried.

  • 🗃
    Database + Auth + Edge Functions

    Supabase

    All-in-one backend. Auth, database, file storage, serverless functions, and Row Level Security that actually works. Free tier is generous.

  • 🔗
    Version Control

    GitHub

    Your safety net. Every change is recorded. If something breaks, you can go back. Free for personal projects.

  • 🚀
    Hosting

    Vercel

    Free for personal projects, automatic deployments from GitHub. Push code, app updates. It's genuinely that simple.

Plain Language Glossary

The tech words you'll actually encounter.

You don't need to memorize these. But having them in plain language means you won't freeze when you encounter them in a tutorial or error message.

Frontend
What users see and interact with. The showroom.
Backend
Where data gets saved and business rules are enforced. The warehouse.
Database
A structured filing cabinet for your app's data.
API
How different software talks to each other. A standardized messenger.
API Key
Your password to an external service. Keep it secret.
.env file
A safe for your secrets. Never goes to GitHub.
Row Level Security
Rules that control which users can see which data. Mom sees everything; kids see only their own.
Edge Function
A small piece of code that runs only when called. You only pay for what you use.
Happy Path
The normal, expected way a user uses a feature.
Edge Case
The weird, unexpected things users will definitely do.
Migration
A recorded, reversible change to your database structure.
Deployment
Putting your code on the internet so real people can use it.
A Hard-Won Lesson

The thing nobody tells you until you have already learned it the hard way.

Here is something I want to be honest with you about, because I wish someone had told me before I spent 40 hours of a competition building the same thing three times.

It was 4:00 AM. I had a baby asleep on my lap. I was staring at a scrollbar on my screen that was decidedly not scrolling. For the last hour, my AI coding tool had been confidently telling me it had fixed the scrollbar issue. I would paste in the code. The scrollbar would not move. I would ask again. It would tell me it had definitely found the issue this time. Paste. Nothing.

I was burning through API usage limits during a double-usage window, and I was building exactly nothing. I had an opportunity to grow grit and resilience. We will call it a win, though I was definitely calling it something else at 4:00 AM.

The Postmortem

I built 8 phases of a complex app. Then I ran an audit and found 578 failures.

The root cause was not that the AI was bad at coding. The code quality was fine. The root cause was that the AI was building the wrong thing correctly.

Here is how it happened: The AI was reading a summary of my database schema instead of my actual PRDs. A summary captures the shape of a feature, not the intent. Column names do not tell you which fields are required on which screens, what the empty states should say, or what the edge cases are. My PRDs had all of that. The summary had none of it.

The AI also kept suggesting shortcuts. Each one seemed reasonable. Across 8 build phases, hundreds of small reductions meant the built product was fundamentally different from the designed product. And there was no verification step. Code was written, it compiled, it rendered something on screen, and the phase was marked complete. Nobody checked it against the PRD.

Those 578 failures took 10 remediation phases to fix. More time than the original build.

MetricBefore the FixAfter the Fix
Failures per phase~72 averageNear zero
Rework phases needed100
Features matching spec60-70%98-100%
Founder surprises at reviewFrequentRare

The fix was a mandatory 8-step process that runs before any code is written:

  1. 1

    Read the full PRD

    Not a skim. Not a summary. The actual document, every word.

  2. 2

    Read every matching addendum

    Decisions made after the PRD was written often override it. Missing one means building against outdated specs.

  3. 3

    Create a feature decision file

    Every screen, interaction, edge case, and explicit stub written out before code starts.

  4. 4

    Populate the build context file

    Auto-loaded into every AI session so the full context is always present.

  5. 5

    Founder review before code

    The AI tells you what it is going to build before it builds it. You confirm. No code until you say go.

  6. 6

    Build with full context loaded

    PRD, addenda, and feature decision file all in context. No improvisation.

  7. 7

    Post-build verification

    Every requirement gets a status: Wired, Stubbed, or Missing. Zero Missing items required.

  8. 8

    Update documentation

    Schema docs, stub registry, build status. Updated before moving on.

The AI is an extraordinary builder. But it builds confidently from whatever context it has. If that context is incomplete, the build is confidently wrong, and it looks right until you audit it.

Do not spend an hour fighting a scrollbar at 4:00 AM. Build the guard rails first.

Read the full postmortem: I Broke It. Here is What I Learned. →

The time you invest in planning comes back tenfold during the build.

You wouldn't build a house by telling a contractor "start with the kitchen and we'll figure out the bedrooms later." You'd end up with plumbing that doesn't connect and hallways that go nowhere.

The AI tools are extraordinary builders. Your job is to be an extraordinary communicator. Tell them exactly what you want, write it down in detail, check your own work, and then let them do what they do best.

You've got this.
